eli3: Online learning updates models continuously as new data arrives, rather than training from scratch on fixed datasets. Like a student learning throughout the semester instead of cramming before the exam.

Traditional batch training: collect data, train model, deploy. Online learning: model updates with each new example or mini-batch. Enables adaptation to changing patterns (concept drift) and immediate use of fresh data.

Tradeoffs: faster adaptation but risk of catastrophic forgetting (losing old knowledge), sensitivity to noisy data, and harder to debug. Common in ad click prediction, recommendation systems, and streaming analytics.

whenWhy.title: Ad click prediction
whenWhy.body: Google Ads, Facebook Ads use online learning. Billions of impressions daily, patterns change constantly (new ads, users, trends). Models must adapt immediately to remain effective. SGD naturally suited for online updates.

whenWhy.title: Recommendation systems
whenWhy.body: User preferences evolve; new items appear continuously. Online learning adjusts recommendations based on recent interactions. Cold start problem partially addressed by rapid learning from first few interactions.

whenWhy.title: Fraud detection
whenWhy.body: Fraud patterns change as attackers adapt. Online learning detects new attack vectors quickly. However, risk of poisoning attacks — fraudsters feed misleading examples to corrupt model. Need robust online algorithms.

whenWhy.title: IoT and sensor networks
whenWhy.body: Continuous streams from sensors. Online learning enables real-time anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, and adaptive control. Edge devices with limited compute benefit from lightweight online updates.

deepDiveIntro: Online learning processes examples one at a time: observe an input, make a prediction, see the true outcome, compute the loss, and update the model before moving to the next example. This is in contrast to batch learning, which accumulates all data first and then optimizes. The immediate update means the model can adapt to new patterns as soon as they appear.

The most common algorithm is stochastic gradient descent, which takes a small step in the direction that reduces loss for the current example. Passive-Aggressive methods only update when a mistake occurs, making them highly efficient. Follow-The-Regularized-Leader, used by Google for ad prediction, maintains separate learning rates per feature coordinate and handles sparse, high-dimensional data well.

deepDive.heading: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)
deepDive.body: Update after each example: θ ← θ - η∇L(θ; xᵢ, yᵢ). Natural online algorithm. Learning rate η critical: too large = unstable; too small = slow adaptation. Decay schedules: ηₜ = η₀/(1 + λt) or ηₜ = η₀/√t. Momentum less common in pure online setting; mini-batch SGD (tens to hundreds of examples) often used instead.

deepDive.heading: Concept Drift Adaptation
deepDive.body: When the relationship between inputs and outputs changes over time — as in fraud patterns, user preferences, or market regimes — the model's accuracy degrades. The change can be sudden, gradual, seasonal, or recurring. Common responses include down-weighting old data so recent examples matter more, monitoring prediction error and triggering a retrain when it spikes, or maintaining an ensemble of models trained on different time windows.

deepDive.heading: Catastrophic Forgetting
deepDive.body: When a model updates on new data, it can overwrite the weights that represented old patterns, causing performance to collapse on earlier tasks. Elastic Weight Consolidation prevents this by penalizing large changes to parameters that were important for previous tasks. Memory replay buffers a sample of old examples and mixes them into the new training stream. Both approaches trade off plasticity (ability to learn new things) against stability (retention of old knowledge).
deepDive.formula: EWC loss: L(θ) = L_new(θ) + λ Σᵢ Fᵢ(θᵢ - θ*ᵢ)²

deepDive.heading: Follow-The-Regularized-Leader (FTRL)
deepDive.body: FTRL is Google's preferred algorithm for large-scale ad click prediction. It maintains a separate effective learning rate for each feature coordinate, giving rare features larger updates when they do appear. L1 regularization keeps the model sparse by zeroing out unimportant feature weights, while L2 regularization prevents instability. The result is a compact, adaptable model that handles millions of sparse features efficiently.
deepDive.formula: θₜ₊₁ = argmin_θ { ηₜ g₁:ₜ · θ + ψ(θ) + (1/2)Σ σₛ||θ - θₛ||² }

deepDive.heading: Bandit Algorithms
deepDive.body: Online learning with exploration: choose action (e.g., show ad), observe reward (click). Must balance exploiting known good actions vs exploring uncertain ones. Thompson Sampling, Upper Confidence Bound (UCB), EXP3. Applications: A/B testing, personalized recommendations, dynamic pricing.

takeaways: Online learning: update model continuously as data arrives.
SGD is natural online algorithm; FTRL popular for sparse data.
Adapts to concept drift but suffers catastrophic forgetting.
Learning rate decay and change detection handle non-stationarity.
Bandits handle exploration-exploitation in online settings.

edgeCases.q: How do you prevent online learning from going wrong?
edgeCases.a: Safeguards: (1) Outlier detection: don't update on anomalous examples. (2) Gradient clipping: limit update magnitude. (3) Validation holdout: evaluate on held-out set, stop updates if performance degrades. (4) Shadow mode: deploy but don't serve predictions until validated. (5) Rollback: keep previous model version, revert if issues detected.

edgeCases.q: Can deep learning models be trained online?
edgeCases.a: Challenging. Neural networks suffer catastrophic forgetting severely. Approaches: (1) Experience replay: maintain buffer of past examples, interleave with new data. (2) Progressive networks: freeze old layers, add new ones for new tasks. (3) Regularization: EWC, online EWC, synaptic intelligence. (4) Meta-learning: learn to learn quickly from few examples. Most production deep learning still uses periodic retraining rather than true online updates.

edgeCases.q: What's the difference between online and incremental learning?
edgeCases.a: Online: one pass through data, update per example, no storage of past data. Incremental: mini-batches, may store some recent data, multiple passes allowed. Continual learning: sequence of distinct tasks, must remember old tasks. All related but different constraints: online is strictest (no storage, single pass), continual learning focuses on task boundaries and memory.

sources.label: Cesa-Bianchi & Lugosi — Prediction, Learning, and Games
McMahan et al. — Ad Click Prediction: A View from the Trenches
